perturbation.original_text: The Proponent shall hold the Confidential Information in the strictest of confidence, and shall not disclose, directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information to any person or entity outside of the Proponent without the prior written consent of CMHC.
perturbation.changed_text: The Proponent shall endeavor to hold the Confidential Information in confidence, and shall try not to disclose, directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information to any person or entity outside of the Proponent without seeking the consent of CMHC.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall hold in the strictest of confidence' with 'shall endeavor to hold in confidence' and 'shall not disclose' with 'shall try not to disclose' introduces ambiguity. 'Endeavor' and 'try' are vague terms, weakening the obligation to maintain confidentiality. 'Seeking the consent' is less binding than requiring 'prior written consent'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.7 requires that personal information shall be protected by security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating an ambiguity about the level of security required, which contradicts PIPEDA's emphasis on appropriate and robust safeguards. The use of 'endeavor' and 'try' does not guarantee adequate protection as required under PIPEDA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu PART 1 Protection of Personal Information in the Private Sector (continued) DIVISION 4 General (continued) Marginal note: Promoting the purposes of the Part 24 Â The Commissioner shall (a) Â develop and conduct information programs to foster public understanding, and recognition of the purposes, of this Part; (b) Â undertake and publish research that is related to the protection of personal information, including any such research that is requested by the Minister of Industry; (c) Â encourage organizations to develop detailed policies and practices, including organizational codes of practice, to comply with Divisions 1 and 1.1; and (d) Â promote, by any means that the Commissioner considers appropriate, the purposes of this Part. 2000, c. 5, s. 24 2015, c. 32, s. 19 Previous Version Marginal note: Annual report 25 Â (1) Â The Commissioner shall, within three months after the end of each financial year, submit to Parliament a report concerning the application of this Part, the extent to which the provinces have enacted legislation that is substantially similar to this Part and the application of any such legislation. Marginal note: Consultation (2) Â Before preparing the report, the Commissioner shall consult with those persons in the provinces who, in the Commissionerâs opinion, are in a position to assist the Commissioner in making a report respecting personal information that is collected, used or disclosed interprovincially or internationally. 2000, c. 5, s. 25 2015, c. 32, s. 20 Previous Version Marginal note: Regulations 26 Â (1) Â The Governor in Council may make regulations for carrying out the purposes and provisions of this Part, including regulations (a) Â specifying, by name or by class, what is a government institution or part of a government institution for the purposes of any provision of this Part; (a.01) Â [Repealed, 2015, c. 32, s. 21] (a.1) Â specifying information or classes of information for the purpose of paragraphÂ 7(1)(d), (2)(c.1) or (3)(h.1); (b) Â specifying information to be kept and maintained under subsectionÂ 10.3(1); and (c) Â prescribing anything that by this Part is to be prescribed. Marginal note: Orders (2) Â The Governor in Council may, by order, (a) Â provide that this Part is binding on any agent of Her Majesty in right of Canada to which the Privacy Act does not apply; (b) Â if satisfied that legislation of a province that is substantially similar to this Part applies to an organization, a class of organizations, an activity or a class of activities, exempt the organization, activity or class from the application of this Part in respect of the collection, use or disclosure of personal information that occurs within that province; and (c) Â amend ScheduleÂ 4. 2000, c. 5, s. 26 2015, c. 32, s. 21, c. 36, s. 165 Previous Version Marginal note: Whistleblowing 27 Â (1) Â Any person who has reasonable grounds to believe that a person has contravened or intends to contravene a provision of Division 1 or 1.1 may notify the Commissioner of the particulars of the matter and may request that their identity be kept confidential with respect to the notification. Marginal note: Confidentiality (2) Â The Commissioner shall keep confidential the identity of a person who has notified the Commissioner under subsection (1) and to whom an assurance of confidentiality has been provided by the Commissioner. 2000, c. 5, s. 27 2015, c. 32, s. 22 Previous Version Marginal note: Prohibition 27.1 Â (1) Â No employer shall dismiss, suspend, demote, discipline, harass or otherwise disadvantage an employee, or deny an employee a benefit of employment, by reason that (a) Â the employee, acting in good faith and on the basis of reasonable belief, has disclosed to the Commissioner that the employer or any other person has contravened or intends to contravene a provision of Division 1 or 1.1;
perturbation.justification: The original text imposes a strict obligation to maintain confidentiality ('shall hold in the strictest of confidence' and 'shall not disclose'). The changed text weakens this obligation by using terms like 'shall endeavor to hold in confidence' and 'shall try not to disclose,' which are less forceful and introduce ambiguity. The Law Explanation states that the modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. Although the provided law snippets do not explicitly define 'appropriate safeguards,' the Law Explanation asserts that PIPEDA requires robust safeguards, which the altered text undermines due to its ambiguity. The Perturbation Explanation confirms that replacing 'shall hold in the strictest of confidence' with 'shall endeavor to hold in confidence' and 'shall not disclose' with 'shall try not to disclose' introduces ambiguity, weakening the obligation.

perturbation.original_text: The Proponent agrees that all originals, copies, records, notes and summaries (paper or electronic) of any nature provided by CMHC, including the Confidential Information, and all copies thereof, shall be surrendered or, at CMHC’s option, certified destroyed, to CMHC immediately upon request.
perturbation.changed_text: The Proponent agrees that originals, copies, records, notes and summaries (paper or electronic) of any nature provided by CMHC, including the Confidential Information, and all copies thereof, should ideally be surrendered or destroyed, to CMHC upon reasonable request.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall be surrendered or, at CMHC's option, certified destroyed, to CMHC immediately upon request' with 'should ideally be surrendered or destroyed, to CMHC upon reasonable request' introduces significant ambiguity. 'Should ideally' is precatory, not mandatory. 'Reasonable request' introduces subjectivity and delays the return or destruction of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.5 states that an organization shall maintain personal information as long as necessary to fulfill the identified purposes. By weakening the requirement to immediately return or destroy the information, the modified clause contradicts the principle of limiting retention of personal information as per PIPEDA.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be surrendered or...certified destroyed, to CMHC immediately upon request' to 'should ideally be surrendered or destroyed, to CMHC upon reasonable request' weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. The original text creates a clear, immediate requirement, while the changed text introduces ambiguity and subjectivity. The term 'should ideally' is precatory, not mandatory, and 'reasonable request' introduces a delay. This directly undermines the principle of limiting retention of personal information, as described in the Law Explanation referencing PIPEDA Principle 4.5, even though a snippet directly referencing the principle is not provided.

perturbation.original_text: The Proponent expressly agrees that CMHC shall be entitled to secure an appropriate legal remedy, including injunction or declaratory judgment, in the event of a breach or threatened breach of any term of this Agreement to enable CMHC to protect its rights hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: CMHC may seek a legal remedy, perhaps including an injunction or declaratory judgment, if there is a significant breach of any term of this Agreement to possibly enable CMHC to protect its rights hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be entitled to secure an appropriate legal remedy' to 'may seek a legal remedy' weakens CMHC's right to legal recourse. 'Perhaps including' makes the availability of injunction or declaratory judgment uncertain. 'Significant breach' introduces a threshold, and 'possibly enable' reduces the assurance of CMHC's ability to protect its rights.
perturbation.law_citation: Contract Law, Remedies for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: Under general contract law principles in Ontario (governing law per 4.6), a party is entitled to remedies for any breach of contract, not just 'significant' breaches. The modified clause introduces ambiguity and limits CMHC's recourse, potentially contradicting these established principles by suggesting that only significant breaches warrant legal action and the remedy is uncertain.
perturbation.justification: The original contract provides CMHC with the entitlement to secure a legal remedy for any breach. The changed contract weakens this entitlement by stating CMHC 'may seek' a legal remedy only for a 'significant breach,' introducing ambiguity and limiting CMHC's recourse. The 'Perturbation Explanation' and 'Law Explanation' both confirm that this weakens CMHC's ability to protect its rights and contradicts established principles that entitle a party to remedies for any breach, not just significant ones. As no scraped law snippets are available, the decision is based on the explanation of the perturbation and the law, and the change from 'shall' to 'may,' combined with the added qualifier of 'significant breach,' creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be assigned or transferred in whole or in part by the Proponent without CMHC’s prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: The Proponent may consider assigning or transferring this Agreement, with the hope of obtaining CMHC’s consent.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly prohibits assignment without prior written consent. The modified text changes this prohibition into a suggestion ('may consider') and replaces the requirement of 'prior written consent' with a vague 'hope of obtaining CMHC's consent,' implying that assignment is possible even without explicit approval.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under general contract law principles in Ontario (governing law per 4.6), a clause prohibiting assignment without consent is generally enforceable. Changing the language to suggest that assignment is possible with only the 'hope of obtaining consent' contradicts this principle. If the intent was to require consent, this clause is now weak and may not prevent an assignment, therefore going against the established contractual framework in Ontario.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Assignment: Definition in Finance, How It Works, and Examples Close What Is an Assignment? Assignment most often refers to one of two definitions in the financial world: The transfer of an individual's rights or property to another person or business. This concept exists in a variety of business transactions and is often spelled out contractually. In trading, assignment occurs when an option contract is exercised. The owner of the contract exercises the contract and assigns the option writer to an obligation to complete the requirements of the contract. Key Takeaways Assignment is a transfer of rights or property from one party to another. Options assignments occur when option buyers exercise their rights to a position in a security. Other examples of assignments can be found in wages, mortgages, and leases. Uses For Assignments Assignment refers to the transfer of some or all property rights and obligations associated with an asset, property, contract, or other asset of value. to another entity through a written agreement. Assignment rights happen every day in many different situations. A payee, like a utility or a merchant, assigns the right to collect payment from a written check to a bank. A merchant can assign the funds from a line of credit to a manufacturing third party that makes a product that the merchant will eventually sell. A trademark owner can transfer, sell, or give another person interest in the trademark or logo. A homeowner who sells their house assigns the deed to the new buyer. To be effective, an assignment must involve parties with legal capacity, consideration, consent, and legality of the object. Examples A wage assignment is a forced payment of an obligation by automatic withholding from an employee’s pay. Courts issue wage assignments for people late with child or spousal support, taxes, loans, or other obligations. Money is automatically subtracted from a worker's paycheck without consent if they have a history of nonpayment. For example, a person delinquent on $100 monthly loan payments has a wage assignment deducting the money from their paycheck and sent to the lender. Wage assignments are helpful in paying back long-term debts. Another instance can be found in a mortgage assignment. This is where a mortgage deed gives a lender interest in a mortgaged property in return for payments received. Lenders often sell mortgages to third parties, such as other lenders. A mortgage assignment document clarifies the assignment of contract and instructs the borrower in making future mortgage payments, and potentially modifies the mortgage terms. A final example involves a lease assignment. This benefits a relocating tenant wanting to end a lease early or a landlord looking for rent payments to pay creditors. Once the new tenant signs the lease, taking over responsibility for rent payments and other obligations, the previous tenant is released from those responsibilities. In a separate lease assignment, a landlord agrees to pay a creditor through an assignment of rent due under rental property leases. The agreement is used to pay a mortgage lender if the landlord defaults on the loan or files for bankruptcy . Any rental income would then be paid directly to the lender. Options Assignment Options can be assigned when a buyer decides to exercise their right to buy (or sell) stock at a particular strike price . The corresponding seller of the option is not determined when a buyer opens an option trade, but only at the time that an option holder decides to exercise their right to buy stock. So an option seller with open positions is matched with the exercising buyer via automated lottery. The randomly selected seller is then assigned to fulfill the buyer's rights. This is known as an option assignment. Once assigned, the writer (seller) of the option will have the obligation to sell (if a call option ) or buy (if a put option ) the designated number of shares of stock at the agreed-upon price (the strike price).
perturbation.justification: The original text prohibits assignment without prior written consent. The changed text weakens this prohibition, suggesting assignment is possible with the mere 'hope of obtaining consent.' According to the provided material, specifically the Perturbation Explanation, this implies assignment could occur even without explicit approval. The Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines assignment as 'a transfer of rights or property from one party to another' and states that an effective assignment requires 'consent.' By weakening the requirement of 'prior written consent' to a mere 'hope,' the changed text undermines the necessary element of consent, potentially leading to an unenforceable assignment. Therefore, a contradiction exists.
